title: Chrysanthemum Tea
description: Chrysanthemum Tea is a delicate and fragrant infusion, beloved in Chinese culture for its light, floral aroma and subtly sweet taste. It offers a refreshing and soothing experience, often enjoyed for its cooling properties and as a gentle, naturally caffeine-free beverage.

ingredients.notes: (杭白菊 - Hangbaiju or 贡菊 - Gongju, approximately 10-15g)

instructions.title: Prepare the Chrysanthemum Flowers
instructions.text: Gently place the dried chrysanthemum flowers into a fine-mesh sieve. Rinse them briefly under cool running water for about 10-15 seconds to remove any dust or impurities. Drain well.

instructions.title: Boil Water
instructions.text: Bring 4 cups of filtered water to a full, rolling boil in a kettle or saucepan. Ensure the water is hot enough to properly extract the flavors.

instructions.title: Initial Steep (Rinse)
instructions.text: Place the rinsed chrysanthemum flowers into a heatproof teapot or glass pitcher. Pour about 1/2 cup of the freshly boiled water over the flowers, swirl gently for 10-15 seconds, then immediately discard this water. This step 'wakes up' the flowers and further cleanses them, preparing for the main infusion.

instructions.title: First Infusion
instructions.text: Pour the remaining hot water (about 3.5 cups) over the rinsed chrysanthemum flowers in the teapot/pitcher. Cover the vessel and let the flowers steep for 3-5 minutes. For a slightly stronger brew, you may extend steeping to 7 minutes, but avoid over-steeping to prevent bitterness.

instructions.title: Sweeten the Tea
instructions.text: Add the rock sugar pieces to the hot tea while it is still steeping or immediately after. Stir gently until the sugar dissolves completely. Adjust the amount of sugar to your personal preference for sweetness.

instructions.title: Serve Hot
instructions.text: Carefully strain the tea into individual heatproof cups, ensuring no loose flowers escape, though some prefer to leave a few flowers in for aesthetic appeal. Serve immediately while hot for the best aromatic experience.

instructions.title: Optional Second Infusion
instructions.text: The steeped chrysanthemum flowers can often be re-infused 1-2 more times. Simply add more hot water (about 3 cups) to the same flowers and steep for a slightly longer duration (e.g., 5-8 minutes) for subsequent infusions. The flavor will be milder but still pleasant.

tips: **Ingredient Quality:** Always opt for high-quality dried chrysanthemum flowers, such as Hangbaiju (杭白菊) or Gongju (贡菊), for the best flavor and aroma. They should look plump and have a natural, bright yellow-white color.
**Water Temperature:** Use freshly boiled water that is just off the boil (around 95-100°C / 203-212°F) to properly extract the delicate flavors without scorching the flowers.
**Sugar Adjustment:** Rock sugar is traditional, but you can adjust the sweetness level or use honey, or even omit sugar entirely if you prefer an unsweetened tea.
**Storage:** Brewed chrysanthemum tea can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 2-3 days and enjoyed cold, especially refreshing in warmer weather.
**Serving Suggestion:** Enjoy chrysanthemum tea on its own, or pair it with dim sum, mooncakes, or other light Chinese pastries. It's also a wonderful palate cleanser.
article:
## History of Chrysanthemum Tea

Chrysanthemum tea, known as Juhua Cha (菊花茶) in Mandarin, boasts a rich history spanning over a thousand years in China, with its origins deeply rooted in the Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD). Initially revered for its medicinal properties within Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), it was prescribed to 'clear heat,' 'detoxify the body,' 'calm the liver,' and 'improve eyesight.' Over centuries, its pleasant aroma and refreshing taste transformed it from a mere herbal remedy into a beloved daily beverage, particularly popular during the Song Dynasty when tea culture flourished. Different varieties, like the delicate Hangbaiju from Hangzhou and the more robust Gongju from Huangshan, became celebrated for their distinct characteristics, making chrysanthemum tea an enduring symbol of Chinese culture and wellness.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Chrysanthemum Tea is designed for authenticity and optimal flavor extraction. The crucial initial rinse step effectively removes any lingering dust or impurities from the dried flowers, ensuring a clean, pure taste. Using just-boiled filtered water at the correct temperature is vital for gently coaxing out the flowers' delicate floral notes without imparting any bitterness. The short steeping time prevents over-extraction, preserving the tea's light body and fragrant aroma. The addition of traditional rock sugar provides a subtle sweetness that perfectly complements the natural floral profile, enhancing the overall soothing and refreshing experience without overpowering the star ingredient.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To achieve the perfect cup of Chrysanthemum Tea, source your dried flowers from a reputable Asian grocery store or tea shop; look for flowers that are whole, vibrant, and aromatic. The 'waking up' rinse (Step 3) is paramount for a clear, pristine brew. While rock sugar is authentic, feel free to experiment with honey or even go unsweetened based on personal preference. Avoid pressing or squeezing the steeped flowers, as this can release bitter compounds. If enjoying cold, brew a slightly stronger batch, then chill it thoroughly and serve over ice. For an added touch, you can steep a few goji berries (枸杞子) alongside the chrysanthemums for a hint of natural sweetness and extra health benefits.

## Serving Suggestions

Chrysanthemum Tea is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways. Traditionally, it's served hot, especially during cooler months, as a comforting and warming beverage. Its light, floral profile makes it an excellent accompaniment to rich or savory Chinese dishes, such as dim sum, spring rolls, or stir-fries, as it acts as a wonderful palate cleanser. During warmer seasons, chill the brewed tea and serve it over ice with a slice of lemon or a few mint leaves for a truly refreshing drink. It also pairs beautifully with lighter desserts like mooncakes or fresh fruit, offering a gentle, aromatic counterpoint to sweetness. For a traditional afternoon experience, serve it in small, delicate Chinese teacups.
